step_by_step_thinking:
Tool Call: retrieve("percentage of teeth showing second mesiobuccal canal visible without magnification")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

After reviewing the retrieved results, none explicitly state the percentage of cases where the second mesiobuccal canal is visible via naked eye. While several references mention challenges in visualizing canals, limitations in access, and reliance on magnification or imaging techniques, no quantitative data supports options B (90–95%), D (40–55%), or C (30%) for visibility of the second mesiobuccal canal. There remains a lack of specificity linking visibility percentages directly to the second mesiobuccal canal. Therefore, despite prior reasoning suggesting option A (62–70%) was plausible, current findings do not confirm this value either. All sources focus broadly on technical difficulty, need for magnification, or imaging modalities—not on exact statistical prevalence of naked-eye observability.
Given that no source provides definitive support for any specific percentage—including those previously proposed—I conclude that the evidence fails to substantiate any of the listed choices definitively. Thus, my agreement stands conditional upon absence of contradictory evidence.
\boxed{A}
Please correct me if I am wrong.

Question: 2nd Mesiobuccal canal can be seen from naked eyes in what percentage of cases
Options:
A: 62-70%
B: 90-95%
C: 30%
D: 40-55%

You called: retrieve("percentage of teeth showing second mesiobuccal canal visible without magnification")
Retrieved Evidence:
[1] Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Indications
The UltraAC is indicated for anterior teeth with intact crowns and minimal structural loss, particularly when preoperative imaging confirms a single straight canal. These cases often benefit from the use of high magnification or guided access methods to maintain precision while preserving as much tooth structure as possible.
[2] Histology, Tooth -- Clinical Significance
Lateral canals can be present in different numbers in a certain tooth and cause problems during endodontic or root therapies. Radiopaque materials can be used to indicate the number and position of these canals.
[3] Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Advantages and Disadvantages
Conservative access cavity Focuses on preserving the pericervical dentin and part of the pulp chamber roof, while allowing adequate canal access. Advantages: Enhances tooth strength compared to TradAC. Disadvantages: Slightly limited visual access may be required for effective cleaning, which may necessitate the use of magnification. [5]
[4] Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Extraction of Teeth
CBCT imaging must only be indicated in specific cases where the operator has a clinical question that panoramic or intraoral radiographs cannot answer. [17] Up-to-date data shows that using CBCT before removing mandibular third molars does not reduce nerve injury or lead to better patient outcomes than panoramic radiographs. [17]
[5] Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Technique -- Traditional technique complications
Craniocervical perforation may develop when the convenience extension is left unfinished before the bur shaft enters the canal space. [63] [61] [62] Pulp debris left behind during preparation may also discolor the crown, especially when the access cavity lacks proper incisal extension and lies too close to the gingival margin. [63] [62] [40] Misjudgment of the tooth's lingual-axial or mesial-axial inclination can result in abrasion of the labial or distal wall during instrumentation. [63] [61] [62] Failure to complete the convenience extension can create a ledge at the apical-labial curve, where the instrument shaft rests on the shoulder or cavity edge instead of advancing smoothly into the canal. [63] [61] [64]
[6] Physiology, Tooth -- Clinical Significance
Of clinical importance to all medical specialties is the proper numbering of teeth. The numbering of teeth begins from right to left, 1-32, with the upper right third molar (wisdom tooth) being tooth number 1. Moving from right to left, top to bottom, 1-16 involves the upper teeth. Tooth number 17 is the lower left third molar, then traveling from left to right, ending at tooth number 32, the bottom right third molar.
[7] Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- Histopathology
Hyperdontia, when examined histologically, reveals supernumerary teeth with a structure resembling that of a normal tooth. These teeth display clearly defined layers of enamel, dentin, and pulpal tissue. Most supernumerary teeth—particularly mesiodens—appear smaller than standard teeth, yet they maintain the same internal architecture. The enamel, dentin, and pulp layers remain present, though reduced in overall size or proportion. [18]
[8] Cracked Tooth Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
Cracked tooth syndrome poses a significant challenge in general dental practice, often confounded by its diverse symptomatology and elusive diagnosis. Cracked tooth syndrome is characterized by an unknown depth fracture plane traversing the tooth's structure, which can result in occasional biting discomfort or escalate to compromise the tooth's integrity, potentially causing pulp involvement or root surface exposure. Due to its ambiguous nature, clinicians must use adept diagnostic methodologies to ensure accurate treatment planning and thorough patient education. An array of diagnostic tools, including visual examination with magnification, fiber-optic transillumination, and bite tests, assists in localizing and evaluating the extent of the crack. Although radiography may provide some insights, cone beam computed tomography is indispensable in cases where conventional imaging methods are insufficient.
[9] Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Before the Eruption of the Permanent Mandibular First Molar
Even if there are no adjacent teeth, operators should cautiously measure the available mesiodistal dimension, and precise proximal tooth reduction should be performed to prevent unnecessary crown margin overhang. This is particularly pertinent before the first permanent molar erupts distally to the second primary molar (see Image. Stainless Steel Crown in Primary Second Molar). If there is any encroachment in the space required for the eruption of the first permanent molar, it leads to a distorted eruption pathway of the permanent molar.
[10] Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Preparation
Radiographic examination is particularly important when dealing with retained roots. With the increasing availability and popularity of cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT), it has become much easier to determine the specific depth, position, size, curvature, and proximity of remaining root fragments to nearby anatomical landmarks like the maxillary sinus and the inferior alveolar nerve. [14] This is the primary limitation of traditional two-dimensional radiographs, though they are still indicated in the initial assessment of retained roots. Not all exodontia cases require a CBCT evaluation, but it becomes valuable when the anticipated surgery increases the risk of iatrogenic damage. Pre-operative evaluation not only gives the surgeon the means to generate an appropriate diagnosis but also determines the proper course of treatment. [14] The surgeon must exercise sound clinical judgment and base their decision-making on empirical evidence and best practices when treating retained root tips. Lastly, the surgeon must decide if they have the clinical skills and armamentarium necessary to perform the procedure safely and effectively. It is never the wrong answer to refer a patient to a higher level of care or to a provider with known expertise in treating a particular condition. [9]
[11] Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Evaluation
Clinically, teeth with grey discoloration can recover to their original color, become yellowed or remain grey. [18] Teeth with dark discoloration can remain clinically asymptomatic with no radiographic signs of necrosis until natural exfoliation. Root canal treatment is not recommended for discolored teeth unless there are clinical signs of infection of the pulp. [17]
[12] Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Technique -- Access Cavity Basic Principles
A preoperative radiograph offers critical details regarding the shape, size, number, and curvature of root canals. [46] Aligning the bur with the radiograph enables the estimation of the required depth of preparation, thereby reducing the risk of overextension or procedural errors. [47] [46] Thorough excavation of carious tissue and debris must follow, as residual material can promote reinfection within the canal. [47] [48] [49]
[13] Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Extraction of Teeth
Preoperative CBCT before tooth extraction is mainly related to impacted mandibular third molars due to the risk of injuring the inferior alveolar nerve (IAN) during surgery. [8] However, the radiograph of choice before removing mandibular third molars is orthopantomography. This x-ray will provide information about the proximity of the tooth with the IAN. The most common radiographic signs associated with IAN injury are the darkening of the roots of the third molar, interruption of the cortical lines of the canal, and diversion of the canal. [16]
[14] Embryology, Teeth -- Pathophysiology
Dilaceration: It is a bend in the root of the tooth in any direction or deviation from the linear relationship of the crown to its root. The site of bending varies with the position of the tooth affected. In the anterior teeth, the bend presents in the apical third of the tooth, middle third in maxillary first molars, and coronal third in the third molars. Permanent teeth are commonly affected.
[15] Apical Periodontitis -- Prognosis
With microscopes and delicate instruments, periapical surgeries remove the apical portion of the root and fill the root in an apico-coronal direction, known as a root-end resection and root-end fill. The dentist should assess postoperative healing radiographically at 12 months post-treatment. 88% of lesions show evidence of recovery at 12 months postoperatively, compared to only 50% when reevaluated at six months. [20] It is also important to note the lesion's size, shape, and presentation clinically and radiographically. This practice will provide comparative information for any follow-up visits.
[16] Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Surgical Considerations
Panoramic imaging is often the primary modality of choice to investigate the proximity of mandibular third molars to the inferior alveolar canal. If high-risk features are identified, such as loss of cortication, diversion or narrowing of the canal, deflection, darkening and narrowing of the roots, or a bifid root apex, then cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) may be indicated to evaluate the risk and aid treatment planning. [42] Coronectomy may be employed in high-risk cases to decrease the risk of injury to the inferior alveolar nerve by removing the crown to prevent the symptoms of pericoronitis and the risk of caries. [43]
[17] UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.
[18] UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

Dental procedures are directly performed on zuby mléčné, indicating that primary (baby) teeth serve as the specific sites for these dental interventions.
[20] UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early-life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.
[21] UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.
[22] UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2808
Aspiration curettage following delivery or abortion is classified as a form of dilatation and curettage (D&C) performed as an aspiration procedure after delivery or abortion.
[23] UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
The category "diseases of mouth; excluding dental" is classified as a subcategory of "اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِиَّة" (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non-dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. An abnormally opaque structure, identified as a morphologic abnormality, is directly addressed through the extraction of a primary membranous cataract by excision, representing a direct morphological intervention aimed at removing the opaque lesion.

Control of epistaxis is a medical procedure whose direct site of action is the nose (nos).
[27] UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1968
Operative procedure on anterior segment of eye is directly related to the eye (oči), indicating that surgical interventions targeting the front structures of the eye are procedures performed on the ocular organ.
[28] UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single-tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

Procedures on Nose are associated with nasal disorder (اِضطِراب أنفي).

Despite extensive review across various domains including endodontics and imaging technologies mentioned throughout retrieved texts:
No study reports actual statistics confirming how frequently second mesiobuccal canals appear visually accessible without magnification – even among maxillary molars where such anatomy occurs naturally.

raw_response:
Tool Call: retrieve("percentage of teeth showing second mesiobuccal canal visible without magnification")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

After reviewing the retrieved results, none explicitly state the percentage of cases where the second mesiobuccal canal is visible via naked eye. While several references mention challenges in visualizing canals, limitations in access, and reliance on magnification or imaging techniques, no quantitative data supports options B (90–95%), D (40–55%), or C (30%) for visibility of the second mesiobuccal canal. There remains a lack of specificity linking visibility percentages directly to the second mesiobuccal canal. Therefore, despite prior reasoning suggesting option A (62–70%) was plausible, current findings do not confirm this value either. All sources focus broadly on technical difficulty, need for magnification, or imaging modalities—not on exact statistical prevalence of naked-eye observability.
Given that no source provides definitive support for any specific percentage—including those previously proposed—I conclude that the evidence fails to substantiate any of the listed choices definitively. Thus, my agreement stands conditional upon absence of contradictory evidence.
\boxed{A}
Please correct me if I am wrong.

The most common radiographic signs associated with IAN injury are the darkening of the roots of the third molar, interruption of the cortical lines of the canal, and diversion of the canal. [16]
[14] Embryology, Teeth -- Pathophysiology
Dilaceration: It is a bend in the root of the tooth in any direction or deviation from the linear relationship of the crown to its root. The site of bending varies with the position of the tooth affected. In the anterior teeth, the bend presents in the apical third of the tooth, middle third in maxillary first molars, and coronal third in the third molars. Permanent teeth are commonly affected.
[15] Apical Periodontitis -- Prognosis
With microscopes and delicate instruments, periapical surgeries remove the apical portion of the root and fill the root in an apico-coronal direction, known as a root-end resection and root-end fill. The dentist should assess postoperative healing radiographically at 12 months post-treatment. 88% of lesions show evidence of recovery at 12 months postoperatively, compared to only 50% when reevaluated at six months. [20] It is also important to note the lesion's size, shape, and presentation clinically and radiographically. This practice will provide comparative information for any follow-up visits.
[16] Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Surgical Considerations
Panoramic imaging is often the primary modality of choice to investigate the proximity of mandibular third molars to the inferior alveolar canal. If high-risk features are identified, such as loss of cortication, diversion or narrowing of the canal, deflection, darkening and narrowing of the roots, or a bifid root apex, then cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) may be indicated to evaluate the risk and aid treatment planning. [42] Coronectomy may be employed in high-risk cases to decrease the risk of injury to the inferior alveolar nerve by removing the crown to prevent the symptoms of pericoronitis and the risk of caries. [43]
